title: Eva on Blu Ray
wikitext:
[]: Eva on Blu Ray?             Quote Post by facespace » Thu Jul 29, 2010 6:39 pm Have they re-released NGE on Blu Ray? I have it on dvd, but I would love to have a copy of the show in a higher rez and better sound quality.

[]:             Quote Post by KnightmareX13 » Thu Jul 29, 2010 6:51 pm Amazon doesn't show NGE in anything other than regular DVDs and VHS. I think that only Rebuild is in Blu Ray.

[]:             Quote Post by Gamer137 » Thu Jul 29, 2010 6:58 pm KnightmareX13 wrote: Amazon doesn't show NGE in anything other than regular DVDs and VHS. I think that only Rebuild is in Blu Ray.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Thu Jul 29, 2010 8:40 pm Currently, there is no Blu-ray edition of Evangelion or an official announcement of a future release, but it's obvious that there will be one.

[]: Re: Eva on Blu Ray?             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Thu Jul 29, 2010 8:55 pm facespace wrote: Have they re-released NGE on Blu Ray? I have it on dvd, but I would love to have a copy of the show in a higher rez and better sound quality.

[]: Re: Eva on Blu Ray?             Quote Post by Killer Bee » Thu Jul 29, 2010 9:06 pm The Eva Monkey wrote: Blu-Ray of The End of Evangelion on the other hand...

[]:             Quote Post by Guyver Spawn » Thu Jul 29, 2010 9:35 pm A Blu Ray re-release would be nice to see, but I'm not sure how it will turn out on screen. I hear most anime series on Blu Ray are not that much different from the normal DVD's you get.

[]: Re: Eva on Blu Ray?             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Jul 29, 2010 10:26 pm The Eva Monkey wrote: Evangelion was shot on 16mm film, which is insufficient for HD.

[]:             Quote Post by facespace » Fri Jul 30, 2010 12:55 am I thought that 16mm was able to scale a bit higher than 1080P (Like barely). But there are processes to upscale film, they did that for films that are shot on 30mm and are upscaled to 70mm for imax purposes.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Fri Jul 30, 2010 1:30 am There's a lot of threads on the AVS forums about film to digital transfers. Although physically there's enough detail on 16mm to do a 2k scan or higher, the problem is mostly the number of "quality" pixels, which all comes down to the type of film, the speed, the process of the filming, what you are filming, your lenses, the amount of light, whether there's a tripod or handheld, etc. There was one guy who does these transfers and said that he sometimes got less than a megapixel of quality pixels from 16mm motion film, and sometimes it's closer to 6 or 7. This is why they prefer doing 35mm to digital transfers because you're guaranteed to get enough quality pixels for something like 1080 HD. I've never seen it myself, but there are people on AVS that claim the scenes from Morocco in the movie Babel were shot on 16mm film while the rest of the movie was 35. Maybe you can tell the difference.

[]:             Quote Post by Bomby von Bombsville » Fri Jul 30, 2010 2:01 am I haven't done so myself, but check out how The Hurt Locker looks on Blu-Ray. That film was completely shot in 16mm. In fact, it's the only film to win Best Picture to have been done so. Could be a gauge of how Eva would look on Blu-Ray.

[]:             Quote Post by Corb » Fri Jul 30, 2010 4:07 am What I would do for End of Evangelion on Blu... it would probably end up the favorite disc in my entire collection. Too bad the chances of that are practically nil with all the attention on the Rebuilds now. If the original series ever ends up in high def, I think they could make it look good, but it better maintain it's original aspect ratio. Gainax has been good about that though, the Gunbuster Blu maintains it's original ratio - and looks amazing. The pan-n-scan in Evangelion: Death is really blah, imo.

[]:             Quote Post by Hexon.Arq » Fri Jul 30, 2010 4:22 am Hell, with EoE I'd be happy with a DVD that didn't look like it was shot through the sticky white blob in Shinji's hand.

[]:             Quote Post by facespace » Fri Jul 30, 2010 9:22 am Bomby von Bombsville wrote: I haven't done so myself, but check out how The Hurt Locker looks on Blu-Ray. That film was completely shot in 16mm. In fact, it's the only film to win Best Picture to have been done so. Could be a gauge of how Eva would look on Blu-Ray.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Fri Jul 30, 2010 1:13 pm facespace wrote: Well I am surprised that haven't tried something about up scaling the video.

[]: Re: Eva on Blu Ray?             Quote Post by Halicat » Fri Jul 30, 2010 1:51 pm Killer Bee wrote: Does Manga Entertainment still have the rights to EoE? Or are we now waiting for Funimation to pick it up?

[]:             Quote Post by Bomby von Bombsville » Fri Jul 30, 2010 3:18 pm facespace wrote: Except for the extreme slow motion explosion in the beginning of the movie. That was shot on a phantom camera all digital.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Jul 30, 2010 7:56 pm Ornette wrote: There's a lot of threads on the AVS forums about film to digital transfers. Although physically there's enough detail on 16mm to do a 2k scan or higher, the problem is mostly the number of "quality" pixels, which all comes down to the type of film, the speed, the process of the filming, what you are filming, your lenses, the amount of light, whether there's a tripod or handheld, etc. There was one guy who does these transfers and said that he sometimes got less than a megapixel of quality pixels from 16mm motion film, and sometimes it's closer to 6 or 7. This is why they prefer doing 35mm to digital transfers because you're guaranteed to get enough quality pixels for something like 1080 HD.

[]:             Quote Post by facespace » Sat Jul 31, 2010 9:44 pm And that I why I always shoot RAW files.

[]:             Quote Post by KnightmareX13 » Thu Jul 29, 2010 6:51 pm Amazon doesn't show NGE in anything other than regular DVDs and VHS. I think that only
